coredata.dc:title: Architecting of an aptasensor for the staphylococcus aureus analysis by modification of the screen-printed carbon electrode with aptamer/Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2
coredata.dc:creator: Ghalkhani, Masoumeh
Sohouli, Esmail
Khaloo, Shokooh Sadat
Vaziri, Mohammad Hossein
coredata.dc:description: Given the many issues bacterial infections cause to humans and the necessity for their detection, in this work we developed a robust aptasensor for prompt, ultrasensitive, and selective analysis of staphylococcus aureus bacterium (S. aureus). A nanocomposite of Ag nanoparticles, chitosan, graphene quantum dots, and nitrogen-doped TiO2 nanoparticles (Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2) was synthesized, and thoroughly characterized by XRD, FT-IR, and FE-SEM spectroscopic methods. The surface of screen-printed carbon electrodes modified with Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2 nanocomposite was utilized as a compatible platform for aptamer attachment. The aptasensor accurately determined S. aureus in the dynamic range of 10–5 × 108 CFU/mL with detection limit of 3.3 CFU/mL. The monitoring of the practical performance of aptasensor in human serum samples revealed its superiority over the conventional methods (relative recovery of 96.25–103.33%). The Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2-based aptasensor offers facile, biocompatibility, good repeatability, reproducibility (RSD = 3.66%), label free and stabile strategy for sensitive S. aureus analysis free from biomolecules interferences in actual specimens.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Staphylococcus aureus
Aptamer
Electrochemical
Graphene quantom dot
Silver nanoparticles

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Pharmaceutical and personal care products (PPCPs) treatment and sensing by 2D carbon nanomaterials; challenges and perspectives, edited by Hassan Karimi-Maleh, Yasin Orooji and Mehmet Lutfi Yola

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Shahid Beheshti University of Medical Sciences
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Shahid Beheshti University of Medical Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this proposal by the Shahid Beheshti University of Medical Sciences with grant No: 25547 . The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of this work by the Shahid Rajaee Teacher Training University , Tehran, Iran. In addition, this proposal has the ethical code of IR. SBMU.PHNS.REC.1399.153.

The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this proposal by the Shahid Beheshti University of Medical Sciences with grant No: 25547. The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of this work by the Shahid Rajaee Teacher Training University, Tehran, Iran. In addition, this proposal has the ethical code of IR. SBMU.PHNS.REC.1399.153.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FT-IR spectrum and (B) XRD pattern of (a) NTiO2, (b), Ag–Cs/NTiO2, and (c) Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2.
Fe-SEM images of NTiO2 (A), Ag–Cs/NTiO2 (B and C), and Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2 (D).

(A) CVs and (B) nyquist plots of the SPCE recorded at consecutive stages of the biosensor assembly process: (a) SPCE, (b) NTiO2/SPCE, (c) Ag–Cs–NTiO2/SPCE, (d) Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2/SPCE, (e) Apt/Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2/SPCE, (f) 104 CFU/mL S. aureus/Apt/Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2/SPCE. All experiments were performed in 0.1 M KCl sample containing 1.0 mM K4Fe(CN)6/K3Fe(CN)6 (1:1); Sweep rate 100 mV/s, the frequency range 0.1 Hz–10 kHz, incubation time 90 min.
DPVs of Apt/Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2/SPCE recorded after different incubation times.
(a) DPVs of the Apt/Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2/SPCE for 10 to 50 × 107 CFU/mL S. aureus, and (b) Plot of the ΔI pa vs. Log [S. aureus].

(A) The bar chart drawn by DPVs response of aptasensor after incubation with target analyte and some interferences (106 CFU/mL). (B) Reproducibility study of the aptasensor for 106 CFU/mL S. aureus on four constructed electrodes.
A comparison of the analysis performance of S. aureus aptasensors.
S. aureus measurement in biological fluids with Apt/Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2/SPCE.

head.ce:title: Architecting of an aptasensor for the staphylococcus aureus analysis by modification of the screen-printed carbon electrode with aptamer/Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shahid Beheshti University of Medical Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Given the many issues bacterial infections cause to humans and the necessity for their detection, in this work we developed a robust aptasensor for prompt, ultrasensitive, and selective analysis of staphylococcus aureus bacterium (S. aureus). A nanocomposite of Ag nanoparticles, chitosan, graphene quantum dots, and nitrogen-doped TiO2 nanoparticles (Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2) was synthesized, and thoroughly characterized by XRD, FT-IR, and FE-SEM spectroscopic methods. The surface of screen-printed carbon electrodes modified with Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2 nanocomposite was utilized as a compatible platform for aptamer attachment. The aptasensor accurately determined S. aureus in the dynamic range of 10–5 × 108 CFU/mL with detection limit of 3.3 CFU/mL. The monitoring of the practical performance of aptasensor in human serum samples revealed its superiority over the conventional methods (relative recovery of 96.25–103.33%). The Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2-based aptasensor offers facile, biocompatibility, good repeatability, reproducibility (RSD = 3.66%), label free and stabile strategy for sensitive S. aureus analysis free from biomolecules interferences in actual specimens.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most prevalent human pathogens, Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) can generate a wide diversity of human infections such as pseudomembranous enteritis, respiratory and some systemic diseases (Tong et al., 2015). It is a serious cause of bacteremia, staphylococcal food poisoning (Argudín et al., 2010), infective endocarditis, and skin and tissue infections, (Tong et al., 2015). With the outbreak of COVID-19 pandemic in the last two years, the deadly role of this bacterium has become more apparent. Infection with S. aureus has put those with coronavirus heart disease at greater risk. The same is true of animals. S. aureus can occur as a secondary bacterial infection in patients infected with COVID-19 and can cause severe cavitating pneumonia, as with influenza. Determination of S. aureus is very important because in the setting of a patient severely ill with COVID-19 pneumonitis, S. aureus may cause bacteremia or secondary pneumonia that are associated with high mortality. Prescribing anti-S. aureus with appropriate penetrating power to the lungs and/or antitoxin synthesis impact such as linezolid and clindamycin is important in the treatment of patients with COVID-19 pneumonitis with evidence of cavitary pneumonia (Chandran et al., 2021). Fast recognition of S. aureus in the initial phase of disease is crucial for diminishing intense fatality.

The conventional analysis approaches require the cultivation of bacteria that involve the prolonged incubation process (Albritton et al., 2017; Li et al., 2018), and need at least 12 h of maturation on rigid ambiance to be quantifiable with this technique (Xu et al., 2018). Currently, conventional detection procedures of S. aureus embrace enzyme-connected immunosensor analysis, real-time polymerase chain process (PCR) (Yoon et al., 2018), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Albritton et al., 2017), magnetic particle enhanced surface Plasmon spectroscopy (Rüppel et al., 2018), and chemical, optical, and electrochemical biosensors (Shahdordizadeh et al., 2017; Niu et al., 2018). Nevertheless, these procedures have some constraints, such as high expenses, demanding expensive accurate tools, expert operators, and prolonged processing courses requiring specified markers. Additionally, false-positive outcomes frequently happen in PCR detection (Zhang et al., 2013). Consequently, developing a novel platform with the swift assay ability of S. aureus is highly demanded. Devising biosensors is an alternative strategy for accelerated, sensitive, and accurate detecting of various analytes, especially bacteria (Lee et al., 2015).

Among various biosensors, electrochemical biosensors are preferred due to their low cost, miniaturization ability, portability, rapid synthesis, simple modification, persistence, and the most negligible use of reagent samples (Silva et al., 2018; Karimi-Maleh et al., 2021a, 2022). Additionally, aptamer-based biosensors (aptasensors) have been associated with an electrochemical-based platform to boost the detection and quantification of pathogens, particularly for food safety obligations (Dinshaw et al., 2017). These platforms further raise sensitivity and affinity since aptamers are exceptionally specific to target molecules (Schoukroun-Barnes et al., 2016). The central part of an aptasensor is a natural element such as oligonucleotides, antibodies, and enzymes that interact chemically or physically with the target and customize the electrochemical signals. Single-stranded oligonucleotides regarded as DNA or RNA aptamers are discriminating against the target molecule (Meng et al., 2016). In addition, aptamers are a relevant alternative for the antibodies regarding their simple separation, prolonged durability, and broad spectrum of uses (Ping et al., 2015). The prevalent approach to make DNA aptamer-based electrochemical biosensors relies on the powerful adsorption of DNA molecules on the flat surface via proper interaction (Deng et al., 2014; Ma et al., 2021; Karimi-Maleh et al., 2021b).

In this work, a screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) was employed as a suitable platform for generating electrochemical aptasensor because of its unique properties, such as specific and straightforward modifications, low background current, widespread potential window, high chemical resistance, and low-price (Renedo et al., 2007; Akter et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2019). The usage of nanomaterials as modifier in construction of the electrochemical aptasensors has elevated the loading of aptamers because of their excellent adsorption capability, bio and structural adaptability. They manifest surface influences, minor size outcomes, and perceptible quantum tunneling outcomes emerging different mechanical, magnetic, optical, electrical, and catalytic properties relative to the bulk materials (Baer et al., 2013; Wernsdorfer et al., 1998). The selectivity, reproducibility, and sensitivity of the electrochemical biosensors have been meliorated through electrode modification by nanomaterial species involving nanoparticles (NPs) (Zeinabad et al., 2018; Oliveira and Morais, 2018; Mehmandoust et al., 2021a, 2021b; Hernández et al., 2021) and quantum dots (QDs) (Wang et al., 2017; Faridbod and Sanati, 2019; Mehmandoust et al., 2021c). Gr QDs as zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterials have been acclaimed for their inimitable features such as excellent biocompatility, controllable magnitude, electrocatalytic performance, and response amplifying. They are strong, non-luminescent, chemically persistent, water-soluble, and photostable upon flickering and photobleaching (Faridbod and Sanati, 2019; Mansuriya and Altintas, 2020). They are broadly utilized for optoelectronics, biological, electrochemical, and environmental applications (Hasanzadeh and Shadjou, 2017). Here, a nanocomposite of nitrogen-doped TiO2 (NTiO2), Ag, chitosan (CS), and Gr QDs was prepared as an SPCE modifier. As a semiconductor material, the nano TiO2 decorated with Ag NPs plays a fundamental role in helping the electron transfer by rendering specific extensive surface areas to S. aureus and assisting in the adsorption of S. aureus (Dayakar et al., 2018). Ag–Cs/NTiO2 NPs also have an antibacterial activity that could support the interaction of aptasensor with S. aureus resulting in higher sensitivity to this bacterium. The doping of heteroatoms such as nitrogen in titanium dioxide improves the surface conductivity of nanoparticles and facilitates charge transfer process, thus promotes the electrochemical performance of the electrode for detection (Cai et al., 2020). Nano-sized hydrogel particles (nanogels) composed of a polymer network with chemical or physical crosslinks are a robust substrate for the storage and release of silver nanoparticles. Chitosan (biopolymer) is a biocompatible cationic polysaccharide applicable in composites with high antimicrobial effects. The data show that a simple mixture of silver and chitosan increases the antibacterial activity of both substances, and the combined affinity for bacteria may be improved. Therefore, in this work, Ag NPs loaded in the three-dimensional lattice of chitosan have been used to form the composite and fabrication of the electrochemical sensor (Liu et al., 2021).

Several electrochemical biosensors have been fabricated to detect S. aureus. Abbaspour et al. (2015) assembled an electrochemical immunosensor for S. aureus based on aptamer-conjugated silver nanoparticles that showed linear dynamic range of determination from 10 to 1 × 106 CFU/mL with limit of detection (LOD) equal to 1 CFU/mL (Abbaspour et al., 2015). At the same year, Wang et al. (2015) proposed a DNA-based electrochemical sensor capable of ultrasensitive detection of S. aureus. The protocol relied on isothermal strand-displacement polymerization process that reached LOD of 63 fM and a linear determination range of 0.075–200 pM (Wang et al., 2015). Later, Reich et al. (2017) designed an impedimetric aptasensor using the thiolated aptamer self-assembled to the Au electrode. The attachment of S. aureus to the aptasensor through its surface protein A created a response with LOD of 10 CFU/mL (Reich et al., 2017). In 2018, Ranjbar et al. prepared a nanocomposite utilizing Au NPs, carbon NPs, and cellulose nanofibers. The nanocomposite-coated electrode acted as a suitable platform for embedding S. aureus aptamer. The reported the linear range of 1.2 × 10–1.2 × 108 CFU/mL with LOD of 1 CFU/mL for the constructed aptasensor (Ranjbar and Shahrokhian, 2018). Recently, Cai et al. (2021) applied a molecular switch approach for development of aptasensor. Here, after attachment of S. aureus spices to the aptamer the complementary DNA sequence releases to the solution that initializes the formation of a ssDNA probe. The interaction of ssDNA probe with molecular switch causes dissociation of the triple-helix DNA structure immobilized on the electrode surface, followed by production of electroactive species. Therefore, a turn on sensitive response in induces. This procedure was able to detect S. aureus in expansive dynamic range of 30–3 × 108 CFU/mL and reached to the LOD of 8 CFU/mL (Cai et al., 2021). Here, a S. aureus aptasensor is introduced relying on the aptamer immobilizing on the screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) modified with Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2 nanocomposite. Notably, the application of silver, titanium oxide and carbon-based nanostructures as electrode modifier has presented outstanding advantages. The presence of Ag NPs are necessary for self-assembly of aptamer strands on the electrode surface. The synthesized Ag NPs tend to agglomerate, leading to a decrease in their chemical activity and effective surface area. The presence of Gr and Cs provides a suitable platform for homogeneous and stable immobilizing of Ag NPs. Moreover, mass production of silver nanoparticles alone has a high cost, so in order to reduce costs in the production of silver nanoparticles and increase the efficiency of a small amount of nanoparticles, nanocomposites of these materials were used, which is very new in its kind. Preparation of a composite with small amount of silver nanoparticles, and Gr QDs with high surface area and CS with suitable functional groups for adsorption of Ag ions will provide efficient electrode modifier and as a result a high performance electrochemical sensor.

Titanium tetraisopropoxide (≥99.99%), acetic acid glacial, (≥99.99%), urea (≥99.5%), chitosan (high purity), silver nitrate (≥99.9%), citric acid (≥99%), sodium borohydride (≥98.0%), sulfuric acid (95%), and other utilized materials were pure and purchased from Merck or Sigma-Aldrich Co. In all electrochemical experiments, a buffer solution prepared by 0.1 M mixture of Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4 species (BPS, pH 7.4) was consumed. The thiol functionalized DNA aptamer with basic sequence to distinguish the S. aureus was bought from Takapozist Co. representation in Tehran, Iran. This aptamer with following sequence was consumed after dilution with sterilized deionized water:
5′–SH–TCG GCA CGT CAG TAG CGC TCG CTG GTC ATC CCA CAG CTA CGT C-3′

All the electrochemical tests were conducted using a μAutolab instrument equipped with the NOVA software. Screen printed carbon electrodes (SPCE) fabricated by DropSens DRP 110 containing two working and counter carbon electrodes and a silver pseudo-reference electrode were utilized. The employed apparatus to adjust the pH of solutions, to prepare nanomaterials dispersions, and to characterize the synthesized nanostructure and the surface of the modified electrodes were like our previous work (Sohouli et al., 2022).

The Escherichia coli (E. Coli), Shigella flexneri (S. flexenri), S. aureus, and salmonellen (Salmon) bacteria were studied as model bacterial strains. These bacteria were grown on the culture nutrient agar plates. The bacteria inoculation was done in LB Broth into a shake flask at a temperature of 37 °C for 24 h. After that, bacteria were gently washed from the plate using sterile distilled water. The collected mixture was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 5 min to separate liquid remains of the medium from bacteria. The numbers of bacteria were quantified via a plate count method. It is the standard method used in microbiology and it consists of growing the bacteria in a nutrient culture Petri dish and counting developed colonies. The suspension was serially diluted to facilitate the counting of the exact bacteria number. 1 mL of each diluted solution was transferred to plate count agar and after incubation under aerobic conditions at the temperature of 37 °C for 24 h, colonies were counted manually using a pencil and a click-counter to determine the exact number of colony-forming units per milliliter (CFU/mL) (McFarland, 1907; Ma et al., 2014).

For the synthesis of Gr QDs, 2 g citric acid was put into a 25 mL crucible and heated to 210 °C for 1 h with a Teflon-lined autoclave. 5 mL of Titanium tetraisopropoxide was transferred to a beaker followed by a dropwise addition of 9 mL of acetic acid. Next, 5 mg urea was added to supply nitrogen as dopant and the solution was stirred for 2 h. The obtained solution was transferred to Teflon-based autoclave, sealed, and then heated up to 120 °C during 24 h. Subsequent to cooling the autoclave at room temperature, the nitrogen doped TiO2 (NTiO2) was collected as product that was washed with D. I water and ethanol, sequentially. Finally, the collected precipitate by centrifuging was dried at an oven. Afterward, 200 mg of the synthesized NTiO2 was dispersed in 100 mL of D. I water, 10 mg silver nitrate was added followed by addition of 10 mL of sodium borohydrate (0.2 g/L), and the stirring was continued for 4 h. Then, 2 mL of the chitosan (0.05 w/w acetic solution) was added and the solution was stirred for 2 h followed by 20 min ultrasonication. Finally, the resulted product was washed with D. I water and ethanol, sequentially, and dried at 45 °C. This product was named as Ag–Cs–NTiO2. Afterward, 200 mg of the synthesized NTiO2 was dispersed in 100 mL of D. I water, 50 mg silver nitrate was added followed by addition of 10 mL of sodium borohydrate, and the stirring was continued for 4 h. Then, 2 mL of the chitosan (0.05 w/w acetic solution) and 10 mg of Gr QDs were added to the solution that then stirred for 2 h followed by 20 min ultrasonication. Finally, the resulted product was washed with D. I water and ethanol, sequentially and dried at 45 °C. This product was named as Ag–Cs-Gr QDs-NTiO2. The Ag–Cs–NTiO2 composite was prepared with the same procedure without addition of Gr QDs.

At first, the suspensions of NTiO2, Ag–Cs–NTiO2, and Ag–Cs-Gr QDs-NTiO2 with concentration of 1 mg/mL were prepared under ultrasonication. The surface of commercially purchased SPCEs was activated by applying chronoamperometry technique under potential of 1.6 V for 150 s in the presence of 50 μL sulfuric acid injected onto its surface. After, thoroughly washing the electrode surface, 10 μL of the desired modifier suspension was poured over the SPCE surface and let dry at room condition. The fabricated modified SPCE were names as NTiO2/SPCE, Ag–Cs–NTiO2/SPCE, Ag–Cs-Gr QDs-NTiO2/SPCE depending on the utilized nanomaterial suspension. Then, 20 μL of the thiolated aptamer was poured over the electrodes surface and kept it at 4 °C for one night to create self-assembled monolayer of aptamer at the electrodes surface. To remove unreacted and physically adsorbed aptamer species, the electrodes surface was thoroughly rinsed with D. I water. As prepared aptasensors were named as Apt-Ag-Cs-Gr QDs-NTiO2/SPCE.

The Apt-Ag-Cs-Gr QDs-NTiO2/SPCE was incubated for 90 min (as the optimal time) at room temperature in the PBS containing desired bacterium concentration. Washing with D. I water was done to eliminate the unhybridized bacteria. The characterization and quantification analysis were conducted by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and differential pulsed voltammetry (DPV) methods, respectively. The variation in the peak current of DPVs of 1 mM ferricyanide ion in 0.1 M KCl solution recorded in the presence and absence of bacteria species was considered as a quantitative signal (equation (1)).
(1) ΔI = I (aptamer - S. aureus) – I (aptamer)

Regarding bacterial pathogenicity in humans, blood serum samples were tested as actual specimen to assay the aptasensor proficiency toward S. aureus. The proteins in human blood can adsorb on the aptasensor and cause its fouling. So, by addition of 2% (v/v) methanol to the real specimens and 10 min centrifuging at high speeds (>5000 rmp) the proteins were agglomerated and separated from the solution. For analysis, the supernatant of the centrifuged samples was diluted 10 times with PBS, and then spiked with the desired values of the bacteria stock solution.

FT-IR spectrum was used to characterize the synthesized nano NTiO2, Ag–Cs/NTiO2, and Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2. Appearance of a peak at 3417 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum NTiO2 is related to the O–H groups, Fig. 1 A–a. The peak at 1624 cm−1 and bands below 500 cm−1 originates from the vibration of Ti–O–Ti bonds and the NH-bending vibration, respectively (Chainarong et al., 2011; Chellappa et al., 2015). Observation of a peak at 3423 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of Ag–Cs/NTiO2 corresponds to the vibration of the N–H and O–H groups, Fig. 1A and b. The peaks at 2921 and 2852 cm−1 belong to the C–H stretching vibrations indicating the presence of hydrocarbon –CH2- groups of the CS structure. A peak at 1470 cm−1 originated from the Ag bonding to the amide group on chitosan. The peaks near the 650 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of Ti–O–Ti (Lin et al., 2015). Fig. 1c shows the FTIR spectrum of Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2 nanocomposite. The peak at 3426 cm−1 corresponds to hydroxyl groups and the stretching vibrations of C–H bond are observed at 2921 and 2852 cm−1. Peak of the aromatic CC bending and CO stretching vibrations of the Gr QDs were observed at 1630 cm−1, Fig. 1A–c. The peak at 1547 cm−1 is due to the N–H deformation vibration of chitosan. Two peaks at 1692 cm−1 and 1516 cm−1 are related to the Ag bonding to the amide group on chitosan.

Analysis of X-ray diffraction patterns was carried out to determine the structure of nano NTiO2, Ag–Cs/NTiO2, and Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2. The peaks at 2θ = 25.23°, 37.56°, 47.89°, 53.8°, 55.2° and 62.7° in the XRD pattern of NTiO2 (Fig. 1B–a) are related to (101), (004), (200), (105), (211) and (204) planes, respectively (ICSD no. 9852) (Huang et al., 2018). The peaks related to NTiO2 along with the peaks in 2θ = 25.0° and 37.9° in the X-ray spectrum of Ag–Cs/NTiO2 nanocomposite (Fig. 1B–b) indicate the presence of TiO2 and AgNO3 trapped in the polymeric structure of the CS (Taspika et al., 2020). The higher amount of the TiO2 and AgNO3 trapped in the Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2 nanocomposite led to appearance of peaks at 25.3° and 37.8°, respectively. However, the Gr QDs peaks were not observed in XRD spectra due to much less amount of Gr QDs in the nanocomposite compared to other components.

The morphology of the synthesized nanomaterial was evaluated by a scanning electron microscope. Fig. 2 A shows a homogenous and spherical morphology for the NTiO2 NPs with the average diameter of about 25 nm. Fig. 2B and C demonstrate the heterogeneously deposited spherical shaped Ag and TiO2 NPs on the surface of chitosan. The fine spherical particles of Gr QDs with mean size of ∼19 nm are observable in the SEM image of Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2 nanocomposite, Fig. 2D. Based on the EDS mapping, Fig. 3 , 42.98% and 28.15% were estimated for the Ti and Ag, respectively, in the Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2. The presence of O (23.82%) and C (2.8%) is related to the Gr QDs and chitosan species. In addition, the existence of N (2.17%) is related to the NH2 groups in chitosan.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry methods were applied to study the performance of the electrochemical aptasensor prepared at each stage of the electrode modification process in a potassium chloride solution (0.1 M) containing K4Fe(CN)6/K3Fe(CN)6 (1 mM). The CV recorded by a bare SPCE showed a quasi-reversible redox waves with peak-to peak segregation of ∼200 mV, Fig. 4 A–a. The EIS response of the bare SPCE revealed a slow electron transfer process at the interface of electrode/electrolyte with a charge transfer resistance (Rct) amount of ∼1.24 kΩ, Fig. 4B–a.

After SPCE modification with nano NTiO2, the potential difference (ΔE p) between redox peaks of the ferro/ferricyanide couple was decreased to 160 mV accompanied with the peaks current increment, Fig. 4A and b. Addition of the Ag–Cs nanoparticles and Ag–Cs-Gr QDs to the NTiO2 layer decreased the ΔE p to less than 120 and 90 mV, respectively. Enlarged current (I pc/I pa ≈ 1) beside the decrement of the potential separation of the redox peaks indicate the effective facilitation of the electrode process. The recorded nyquests spectra conformed the fast and kinetically improved electron transfer process as the semicircle radius decreased largely along with the addition of NTiO2, Ag–Cs/NTiO2, and Ag–Cs-Gr QDs-NTiO2 composites to the electrode surface, Fig. 4B.

High electrical conductivity of the Gr QDs and Ag nanoparticles with their fast charge transfer ability improved the electrochemical effect of NTiO2 nanopartlces. The chitosan effectively provided strong interactions between the components of the nanocomposite, which prolonged its stability and boosted its adherence to the SPCE surface. The peaks current reduced sharply after the aptamer assembly process revealing its successful self-assembling on the Ag–Cs-Gr QDs-NTiO2/SPCE surface. Meanwhile, by contacting the Apt/Ag–Cs-Gr QDs-NTiO2/SPCE with S. aureus bacteria, the ΔE p increased to 220 mV while the peaks current significantly decreased. These observations indicate the prevention of the electrochemical process as the consequence of the strong interactions between aptasensor and S. aureus molecules. The Fig. 4B (e, f) illustrates the high resistance and inferior kinetics of the charge transfer phenomena. The presence of S. aureus bacteria in the electrode surface hinders the diffusion of the electroactive probes species to the surface of the Apt/Ag–Cs-Gr QDs-NTiO2/SPCE. The nanocomposite of Ag–Cs-Gr QDs-NTiO2 inherits abundant active sites and provides a large surface area for attachment of aptamers to the SPCE surface, thus significantly motivating the sensing potential for the S. aureus bacteria.

To achieve the satisfactory performance of the constructed aptasensor, the impact of sensing parameters were optimized. The solution pH is a key parameter that affects the aptasensor efficiency. Regarding to previous research (Zargar et al., 2018), the biological condition of pH 7.4 was considered through whole experiments. The desirable affinity of the Apt sequence and Ag nanoparticles is established in neutral pHs. Notably, the probable damage of the Apt sequence in strong alkaline or acidic situation leads to inferior sensing responses. To ensure of the adequate attachment of the Apt sequence on the Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2/SPCE, the self-assemble monolayers was proceeds overnight at 4 °C.

The incubation time during which the bacterium species attached to the aptasensor was optimized by sensing 104 CFU/mL bacterium after its hybridization with Apt/Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2/SPCE under 15–150 min incubation time. The aptasensor response was monitored by DPV technique, Fig. 5 . The peak current of the DPVs decreased upon bacterium recognition until 90 min that then kept almost unchanged and showed a stable value. Hence, the incubation time of 90 min was applied in further experiments.

The analysis performance of the Apt/Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2/SPCE was explored by recording the DPV response of different S. aureus standard solutions under optimized situation, Fig. 6 . The peak current of DPVs decreased upon addition of S. aureus concentration because restricted reaching of the probe species to the electrode surface and diminished the charge transfer intensity. The decrement of the sensing response was proportional to the logarithm of the S. aureus concentration (10–5 × 108 CFU/mL). A limit of detection (LOD) and a limit of quantification (LOQ) of 3.3 CFU/mL and were achieved based on S/N = 3, respectively.

Diverse aptasensors have been developed for S. aureus analysis. Their sensing characteristics were compared in Table 1 . The wider linear range and lower LOD of the Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2 based aptasensor are superior to previously reported works.

The selectivity, response time, repeatability, reproducibility, and stability are important figures of merit of any sensor. The response selectivity of the Apt/Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2/SPCE toward S. aureus determination was evaluated by recording the DPV of S. aureus and some potential interferences such as E. Coli, Salmon, and S. flexenri bacteria, separately. The bar chart of the variation of the peak current indicates distinguish and sharp response to the S. aureus with a weak and negligible response to other bacteria, Fig. 7 A. The selective binding of the S. aureus to the surface of Apt/Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2/SPCE in the absence of sensible nonspecific adsorption led to selective recognition. Furthermore, a series of aptasensors individually constructed based on optimized strategy. Then, they were employed for electroanalysis of a S. aureus solution (106 CFU/mL) by DPV method, Fig. 7B. The RSD value less than 3.66% for the sensing response of four separate aptasensor confirmed acceptable reproducibility of the sensor construction.

The stability of the sensing layer developed on the surface of SPCE was tested by performing successive CVs in the ferrocyanide ion solution (1 mM in 0.1 KCl) using constructed Apt/Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2/SPCEs. The CV shape did not changed and the redox peaks potential of the ferro/fericyanide ions remained stable while the peaks current changed less than 10% of its initial value even after 100 consecutive scans. These observations reflected admirable stability and robustness of the aptasensor in steady testing. The gathered results of the these performed surveys demonstrated an outstanding durable, facile, selective and reproducible result of the Apt/Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2/SPCEs platform applicable for accurate S. aureus analysis.

The practical performance and feasibility of the Apt/Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2/SPCE was examined by determining the recovery percentage of the spiked standard S. aureus in human serum samples. The recovery values listed in Table 2 were in the range of 96% with RSD of less than 103% indicate satisfactory distinguishing of S. aureus in biological fluids by constructed aptasesnor and its capability for routine applications.

The S. aureus selective aptamer was attached to the Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2/SPCE through self-assembly procedure. The appearance of the characteristic peaks at 25.3° and 37.8° in the XRD spectrum of the Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2 revealed the entrapment of TiO2 and AgNO3 in the polymeric structure of the CS. The values of 42.98% and 28.15% were estimated for the Ti and Ag content in the Ag–Cs-Gr QDs/NTiO2 nanocomposite based on EDS mapping. Presence of Ag NPs with antibacterial effect besides the Cs improved the aptasensor stability. The fine spherical Gr QDs (d ≈ 19 nm) and NTiO2 NPs (d ≈ 25 nm) synergistically increased both the active surface area and facilitated electronic conductivity. Therefore, the synergetic impact of nanocomposite constituents together helped to fabricate an efficient platform for aptasensor construction. After optimizing analysis conditions, the oxidation current of the ferrocyanide ion was measured as a biodevice response. The resulting sensitive and selective responses made it possible to measure trace amount of S. aureus in the range of 10–5 × 108 CFU/mL with a low detection limit of 3.3 CFU/mL.

Masoumeh Ghalkhani: Supervision, Conceptualization, characterization, data analysis and manuscript writing. Esmail Sohouli: Sample preparation, Synthesis, electrochemical experiments. Shokooh Sadat Khaloo: Conceptualization, Providing required facilities. Mohammad Hossein Vaziri: Providing some required facilities.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this proposal by the Shahid Beheshti University of Medical Sciences with grant No: 25547. The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of this work by the Shahid Rajaee Teacher Training University, Tehran, Iran. In addition, this proposal has the ethical code of IR. SBMU.PHNS.REC.1399.153.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence detection of total count of Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus on water-soluble CdSe quantum dots coupled with bacteria

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Xue, J. Pan, H. Xie, J. Wang, S. Zhang, Fluorescence detection of total count of Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus on water-soluble CdSe quantum dots coupled with bacteria, Talanta, 77 (2009) 1808-1813.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Yoon et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A highly selective enrichment broth combined with real-time PCR for detection of Staphylococcus aureus in food samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.-H. Yoon, S. Wei, D.-H. Oh, A highly selective enrichment broth combined with real-time PCR for detection of Staphylococcus aureus in food samples, LWT, 94 (2018) 103-110.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zargar et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aptamer-modified carbon nanomaterial based sorption coupled to paper spray ion mobility spectrometry for highly sensitive and selective determination of methamphetamine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Zargar, T. Khayamian, M.T. Jafari, Aptamer-modified carbon nanomaterial based sorption coupled to paper spray ion mobility spectrometry for highly sensitive and selective determination of methamphetamine, Microchimica Acta, 185 (2018) 1-9.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zeinabad et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasensitive interdigitated capacitance immunosensor using gold nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.A. Zeinabad, H. Ghourchian, M. Falahati, M. Fathipour, M. Azizi, S.M. Boutorabi, Ultrasensitive interdigitated capacitance immunosensor using gold nanoparticles, Nanotechnology, 29 (2018) 265102.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zelada-Guillén et al., 2012

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Label-free detection of Staphylococcus aureus in skin using real-time potentiometric biosensors based on carbon nanotubes and aptamers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.A. Zelada-Guillen, J.L. Sebastian-Avila, P. Blondeau, J. Riu, F.X. Rius, Label-free detection of Staphylococcus aureus in skin using real-time potentiometric biosensors based on carbon nanotubes and aptamers, Biosensors and bioelectronics, 31 (2012) 226-232.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhang et al., 2013

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive detection of enteropathogenic E. coli using a bfpA gene-based electrochemical sensor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Zhang, C. Luo, L. Zhong, S. Nie, W. Cheng, D. Zhao, S. Ding, Sensitive detection of enteropathogenic E. coli using a bfpA gene-based electrochemical sensor, Microchimica Acta, 180 (2013) 1233-1240.
